parentQuestion: A patient with a brainstem stroke presents with bilateral loss of pain and temperature sensation over the entire body with preserved fine touch and proprioception. Where is the lesion located?

scaffolds.q: Do pain/temperature and fine touch/proprioception travel in the same pathway?
options: Yes, they use the same tract
No — they travel in separate pathways (spinothalamic vs dorsal columns/medial lemniscus)
They only separate in the cortex
scaffolds.explain: Pain/temp travels in the SPINOTHALAMIC TRACT. Fine touch/proprioception travels in DORSAL COLUMNS → MEDIAL LEMNISCUS. DIFFERENT tracts!

wrongExplains.0: These sensory modalities use completely different tracts.
wrongExplains.2: Both have well-defined pathways in the spinal cord and brainstem.
wrongExplains.3: They're separate from the spinal cord level, not just in cortex.

scaffolds.q: What does the spinothalamic tract carry?
scaffolds.explain: The SPINOTHALAMIC TRACT (anterolateral system) is the dedicated pathway for PAIN and TEMPERATURE from the body.

wrongExplains.0: Fine touch and proprioception use dorsal columns/medial lemniscus.
wrongExplains.2: Motor commands use corticospinal tract; spinothalamic is sensory.
wrongExplains.3: Vision uses optic pathways, not spinothalamic.

scaffolds.q: What does the medial lemniscus carry?
scaffolds.explain: DORSAL COLUMN fibers synapse in medulla, cross, and form the MEDIAL LEMNISCUS — carrying FINE TOUCH, VIBRATION, and PROPRIOCEPTION.

wrongExplains.0: Pain/temp is spinothalamic; medial lemniscus carries discriminative touch.
wrongExplains.2: Motor is corticospinal; medial lemniscus is sensory.
wrongExplains.3: Hearing uses auditory pathways, not medial lemniscus.

scaffolds.q: Where do these two tracts run in the medulla?
options: They run together in the same location
In different parts — spinothalamic more centrally, medial lemniscus more laterally/ventrally
Neither tract goes through the medulla
scaffolds.explain: In the medulla, the tracts run in DIFFERENT POSITIONS. A strategic lesion can damage one while SPARING the other.

wrongExplains.0: They're anatomically separated, which allows selective damage.
wrongExplains.2: Both tracts pass through the medulla.
wrongExplains.3: Both tracts are real and distinct.

scaffolds.q: How could bilateral pain/temp loss occur with preserved fine touch?
options: Impossible — all sensation must be lost together
A central medullary lesion could damage both spinothalamic tracts while sparing medial lemnisci
scaffolds.explain: Because tracts run in DIFFERENT POSITIONS, a CENTRAL MEDULLARY lesion can damage BOTH spinothalamic tracts while SPARING the medial lemnisci.

wrongExplains.0: The anatomical separation allows selective sensory loss.
wrongExplains.2: Central medullary lesion can cause this pattern.
wrongExplains.3: Fine touch is routinely tested clinically.

scaffolds.q: What does the sensory dissociation pattern tell us about localization?
options: The lesion must be where the tracts are still anatomically separated
The lesion must be in the cortex
scaffolds.explain: The DISSOCIATION tells us the lesion is where tracts are SEPARATED. In thalamus, all sensory info converges. In medulla, tracts are still separate.

wrongExplains.0: Sensory dissociation is very useful for localization.
wrongExplains.2: Cortex is where sensations converge; dissociation indicates lower lesion.
wrongExplains.3: Peripheral lesions wouldn't cause this pattern.

scaffolds.q: Why is the lesion bilateral in this case?
options: Because the patient has two strokes
Because a central/medial medullary lesion can affect both spinothalamic tracts at once
scaffolds.explain: The spinothalamic tracts run CLOSE TOGETHER centrally in the medulla. A SINGLE CENTRAL lesion can damage BOTH simultaneously.

wrongExplains.0: A single strategically placed lesion can cause bilateral effects.
wrongExplains.2: Most strokes are unilateral; this is a special anatomical situation.
wrongExplains.3: Unilateral lesions typically cause contralateral sensory loss.

scaffolds.q: What medullary region contains both spinothalamic tracts close together?
options: The tracts are never close together
scaffolds.explain: The CENTRAL/MEDIAL MEDULLA contains both spinothalamic tracts close together. A small lesion here damages both while sparing lateral structures.

wrongExplains.0: Lateral medulla contains different structures (Wallenberg syndrome).
wrongExplains.3: The tracts do run close together centrally.

scaffolds.q: Bilateral pain/temp loss with preserved fine touch = lesion in the ___?
scaffolds.explain: CENTRAL MEDULLA (BILATERAL). The spinothalamic tracts run centrally; a lesion here damages both while sparing the lateral medial lemnisci.

wrongExplains.0: Lateral medulla causes Wallenberg (crossed sensory loss), not bilateral.
wrongExplains.2: Thalamic lesion would affect all modalities together.
wrongExplains.3: Spinal cord lesion pattern would be different.
